name: Cattle of Geryon
description: The Cattle of Geryon were a herd of magnificent red oxen guarded by the three-bodied giant Geryon, his two-headed dog Orthrus, and the herdsman Eurytion. These cattle were renowned for their exceptional beauty and quality, making them an incredibly valuable prize. Obtaining them was the tenth of Heracles' (Hercules') legendary Twelve Labors, a task set by King Eurystheus of Tiryns. To reach Geryon, Heracles journeyed to the far west, likely identified with Erytheia, an island near the Pillars of Hercules (Strait of Gibraltar). After slaying Orthrus, Eurytion, and finally Geryon himself with arrows dipped in the venom of the Lernaean Hydra, Heracles claimed the cattle. The journey back to Eurystheus was fraught with peril, including encounters with various monsters and even interventions by Hera, who sought to hinder Heracles' progress. The cattle were eventually sacrificed to Hera, completing Heracles' tenth labor and further solidifying his heroic status. The Cattle of Geryon represent not just wealth, but also the ultimate challenge of overcoming monstrous obstacles and traversing the boundaries of the known world.
subtitle: Red oxen of a monstrous giant.
origin: Bred and guarded by the giant Geryon on the island of Erytheia.
currentLocation: Sacrificed to Hera; no longer exist.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Tenth Labor
keyMyths.description: The Cattle of Geryon were the prize of Heracles' tenth labor, requiring him to travel to the edge of the world and defeat several monstrous guardians. This labor demonstrated Heracles' strength, endurance, and cunning.

keyMyths.title: Journey to Erytheia
keyMyths.description: Heracles' journey to the island of Erytheia was a perilous one, highlighting the geographical challenges and the dangers lurking at the edges of the known world. He faced many trials along the way.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Comprehensive account of Greek myths, including Heracles' labors.
sources.text: Comprehensive account of Greek myths, including Heracles' labors.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: Historical account incorporating mythological elements, including Heracles' adventures.
